dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 70-year-old female with chest pain-call type: sick person.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What prompted the 911 call today?
She is nonverbal and experiencing chest and abdominal pain with visible tremors.
She is conscious and self-maintaining airway, appears cognitively intact to baseline.
Staff on scene confirms airway is patent and maintainable.
Regular respirations at 16 per minute. No signs of labored breathing.
Radial pulse is palpated but weak. Skin is cool, dry to touch, and lacks surface warmth.
Patient has long-standing Parkinson's, hypertension, schizoaffective disorder, and hyperlipidemia.
No history of drug allergies was provided by on-scene staff.
Callers noted the chest discomfort began approximately 30 minutes ago.
No obvious triggers causing the chest discomfort on clinical exam.
Cannot specify quality of pain due to the patient's nonverbal status.
No pain behaviors lateralizing to limbs or back on clinical exam.
Nonverbal rating not available, but field note suggests a subjective measure around 6.
Consistent pain reported by staff for 30 minutes at onset.
Skin is cool, dry, and intact-no abnormalities noted aside from dryness.
Pupils are equal and reactive-no signs of concern in head or neck.
No obvious abnormalities in the head, mouth, or throat areas.
Clear but diminished bilateral lung sounds; no wheezes or crackles.
Soft and non-tender abdomen-no signs of acute distress.
Back appears atraumatic, and pelvis is intact-no abnormalities identified.
Pulse starts at 64, later becomes 60 and 61 CIM.
Respiratory rate is initially 16 and increases to 18 over time.
Blood pressure is 162/90, followed by 143/82 then 135/76.
First glucose reading is 84, then equipment reads as 0-fix point in progress.
Oxygen saturation remains 95% and then improves to 98%.
EKG findings consistent with sinus bradycardia, right bundle branch block, left anterior fascicular block, and short QT interval.
First IV attempt fails-22G line placed successfully in left arm.
200 mL warmed normal saline infused per hypothermia protocol.
4-lead applied by 20:09, 12-lead ECG available by 20:17.
Pulse remains stable with slight decline-minimal trend changes observed in vitals.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with conduction delays and a short QT interval.
No facial or vocal signs of worsening pain on repeat inspection.
Clinical status unchanged patient continues to be nonverbal.
Following Protocol 5-1 for hypothermia and cardiac arrhythmia.
Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] for cardiac and neurological evaluation.
Patient walked with help into semi-Fowler's and was secured with restraints.
Patient is able to open eyes in response to verbal stimuli.
Patient is unable to respond verbally due to nonverbal status.
Patient has appropriate motor responses to painful stimuli.
Oxygen saturation remains consistently above 95% post VI and remains stable.
+PMSx4 noted on clinical exam-no issues in extremity pulses or sensation.
Patient reports taking amlodipine, metoprolol, levodopa, and several others.
No signs of flaring nostrils or use of accessory muscles-respirations remain even.
No deformities or tenderness found on chest wall inspection.
Patient is alert and oriented to baseline as per field notes.
Airway remains clear, and patient is maintaining with no obstruction.
Asymmetric chest movement not observed, no signs of acute distress in breathing.
No cyanosis noted on extremities; skin color is consistent with baseline.
Atraumatic back noted with no signs of neurological deficits on exam.
Patient is awake, vocalizes mild discomfort but is nonverbal overall.
No signs of rash or bruising on examination of the skin.
No breathing or speech sounds noted-no drainage or obstruction observed.
12-lead ECG shows right bundle branch block and left anterior fascicular block findings.
Work of breathing is minimal and unlabored.
No visible deformities or angulations in extremities.
16 respirations per minute, depth consistent with normal tidal volume.
Visual inspection of oral cavity shows no signs of trauma or bleeding.
Dry and intact skin noted without signs of infection or pressure points.
PERRLA noted during initial anterior inspection of the eye.
Rales, rhonchi, or wheeze not auscultated per field notes.
No signs of localized wheals or color change on body surface.
Airway is patent, maintainable without assistance from EMT or caregiver.
Trachea is midline as per physical exam and history recorded in PCR.
No masses or distension present on palpation and inspection.
Intact pelvis without tenderness or crepitus on movement or pressure.
Patient exhibits coordinated responses when tested with palm pressure.
No signs of confusion or agitation reported by patient or staff during response.
No cyanosis present in lips or nailbeds during evaluation.
Glucose reading repeated with device error-fix point established.
No history of trauma or injury to the head noted in field impression.
No obstruction signs at this time, airway maintained during the primary survey.
No wounds, scabs, or marks on lower extremities on auscultation and palpation.
Respirations are regular and unlabored based on observed behavior.
No temperature fluctuations or jaundice highlighted in recorded findings.
No visible injury to oral cavity, uvula, or pharyngeal structures.
SpO2 remains elevated and consistent with ambient room air in all series.
Nuchal rigidity not detected during head flexion confirmation.
Bowel sounds not elicited but patient denied pain during this process.
As of latest assessment, no abnormal inspiratory or expiratory behavior.
No signs of crepitus or crease lines in back or extremities.
Last solid intake unknown but liquid intake limited, per staff report.
Memory deficits not confirmed despite history of schizoaffective disorder.
Pulse is detected, but volume described as reduced after fluid bolus.
No JVD or jugular pulses noted during charting of field impression.
Warm limb temperatures noted with no signs of peripheral edema.
Airway position is ideal with no signs of deviation or collapse.
No signs of sunken eyes, but xerostomia present, per clinical charting.
No significant fluctuations despite IV administration-trend consistent with normotension.
No signs of lesion or abnormal patches observed during skin inspection.
No focal neurological deficit present at time of secondary assessment.
No sound of wheeze or stridor heard during lung inspection and listening.
No increase in RR noted consistent with a stable ambient body temperature.
No signs of burn or chemical exposure on skin surface, per initial field impression.
Cardiac sounds are regular with no S3 or S4 gallops noted in auscultation.
No signs of cold intolerance or capillary refill beyond normal time limits.
No signs of shock or further instability noted in baseline field assessment.
No history of falling or movement delay beyond documented comorbidities.
Additional EKG analysis shows short QT interval and possible apical infarct findings.
No signs of skin breakdown or pressure sore on inspection and palpation.
